# Decision Records Over Institutional Memory

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Principle

Engineering teams SHOULD record significant technical decisions — the
choice made, the alternatives considered, the constraints that drove the
decision, and the expected consequences — in a durable, version-controlled,
discoverable artifact. Decisions SHOULD NOT live only in Slack threads,
meeting notes, or the memories of the people who were in the room.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C07 (Knowledge Decay)** — Knowledge that exists only in human
  memory decays. People leave, forget, or misremember. A decision that
  was obvious six months ago becomes a mystery when the original authors
  have moved on. Written decision records arrest decay by converting
  ephemeral understanding into persistent, searchable documentation.

## Why This Matters

Without decision records, teams relitigate settled questions. A new
engineer joins and asks "why did we choose Postgres over DynamoDB?" If
the answer lives only in someone's head, the team spends an hour
reconstructing context that could have been a five-minute read. Worse,
if the original decision-maker has left, the team may reverse a sound
decision because they lack the context that justified it. Decision
records are not bureaucracy — they are institutional memory made durable.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Recording every micro-decision creates noise that buries the signal.
The principle targets significant decisions — those that constrain future
options, involve meaningful tradeoffs, or are likely to be questioned
later. Teams must develop judgment about what rises to the level of a
formal record. There is also the maintenance question: decision records
can become stale if the landscape shifts and no one updates them. A stale
decision record is better than no record (it at least captures original
context), but teams SHOULD revisit records when the assumptions behind
them change.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, decisions were recorded in design documents and technical memos
on paper. In 2070, AI agents may automatically capture and index decisions
from engineering conversations. The medium evolves; the principle that
decisions must outlive the people who made them does not.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.
